skill.name: building-mcp-servers
skill.description: Guides teams through secure Model Context Protocol server configuration for Claude Code plugins, including local, HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket integrations.
skill.summary: Build and validate secure MCP server integrations for Claude Code plugins.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, network, and credential alerts, but review shows most are documentation examples or template placeholders. The real risk is contextual: the skill helps create MCP configurations that can launch local processes and connect to remote authenticated services, so users need security review before using generated configs.

medium_findings.title: MCP Stdio Configurations Can Launch Local Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches stdio MCP configurations that start local commands such as node, python, and npx. This is intended for MCP, but generated configs can execute local code when loaded by Claude Code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command fields are explicit and semantically part of MCP stdio integration. No injection path is shown, but executing configured local commands is a real security boundary.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Examples Carry Environment-Based Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill includes HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket MCP examples that attach bearer tokens or API keys to remote endpoints. The values are placeholders, but users may adapt them for live secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and credential patterns are confirmed, but they use environment variable placeholders and example domains. Risk depends on user-supplied endpoints and secret handling.

low_findings.title: Static Secret Alerts Are Placeholder Documentation
low_findings.description: The detected API keys, database URLs, and token references are examples or environment variable placeholders. I found no hardcoded live secret in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text explicitly instructs users not to hardcode credentials and shows environment variable syntax. No evidence found of embedded real credentials.

low_findings.title: Validation Script Reads User-Selected JSON Files
low_findings.description: The validator opens the path supplied on the command line and parses JSON. This is expected behavior for a validator and does not show arbitrary filesystem collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File access is limited to the user-provided validation target. No evidence found of recursive scanning, hidden credential harvesting, or network transfer.

low_findings.title: Insecure URL Examples Are Labeled As Unsafe
low_findings.description: Some HTTP and ws examples are intentionally shown as insecure patterns in security guidance. The surrounding guidance recommends HTTPS and WSS.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are presented as bad patterns and immediately followed by secure alternatives. The validator also warns on non-local HTTP URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configured Local Process Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: MCP stdio servers can start local executables from configuration. Users should review command and args before enabling generated server configs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and expected for stdio MCP servers. It becomes dangerous if a generated or copied config points to untrusted code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Remote Endpoint Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote MCP templates combine external URLs with authorization headers. Users must trust the endpoint before passing environment-based credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The templates use example domains and placeholders, not live exfiltration. The pattern is still sensitive because real deployments attach secrets to remote services.

content.value_statement: MCP server setup can be difficult because transport, authentication, and plugin configuration must align. This skill provides templates, validation guidance, and security checks for Claude Code plugin integrations.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use MCP servers instead of built-in tools.
Compares stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket MCP server types.
Provides reusable MCP configuration templates for common transports.
Documents environment variable handling for API keys and tokens.
Includes a Python validator for MCP configuration files.
Lists security checks for credentials, URLs, paths, and allowed tools.
limitations: Does not implement a full MCP server application by itself.
Requires users to supply real endpoints, secrets, and service requirements.
Validation is heuristic and cannot prove an endpoint is trustworthy.
Examples target Claude Code plugin workflows, not every MCP host.

use_cases.title: Add a local tool server
use_cases.description: Create a stdio MCP configuration that starts a local Node.js or Python helper for a plugin.

use_cases.title: Connect a hosted API
use_cases.description: Choose HTTP, SSE, or WebSocket transport and document the needed authentication variables.

use_cases.title: Review MCP security posture
use_cases.description: Check generated configuration for hardcoded secrets, insecure URLs, absolute paths, and broad tool access.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an MCP transport
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to add an MCP server for a service. Ask me the required questions and recommend stdio, HTTP, SSE, or WebSocket.

prompt_templates.title: Create a secure config
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code plugin MCP configuration for my service. Use environment variables for secrets and explain each required field.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an MCP setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this MCP configuration for security risks. Focus on credentials, URL schemes, paths, and allowed tools.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-server plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an MCP plan for a plugin with local database tooling and a remote API. Include transport choices, secret names, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A developer needs to connect a plugin to a hosted REST API with an API key.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends HTTP transport, an HTTPS endpoint, an authorization header using an environment variable, and README documentation for the key.

output_examples.input: A team wants a plugin to run a local helper script as an MCP server.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends stdio transport, a portable plugin-root path, explicit command review, and validation before publishing.

output_examples.input: A reviewer asks whether an MCP config is safe to publish.
output_examples.output: The skill checks for hardcoded secrets, insecure protocols, absolute paths, missing server types, and overly broad tool access.

best_practices: Review every generated command and endpoint before enabling an MCP server.
Use environment variables or secret managers for all credentials.
Allow only the MCP tools that the plugin truly needs.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode API keys, tokens, passwords, or database URLs.
Do not use plain HTTP or ws for remote MCP services.
Do not auto-allow destructive or administrative MCP tools.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps create and review MCP server integrations for Claude Code plugins.

faq.question: Does it support local MCP servers?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers stdio servers that run local Node.js, Python, or CLI-based tools.

faq.question: Does it support hosted services?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket configurations for remote MCP endpoints.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use environment variables or managed secrets. Do not place real credentials in plugin files.

faq.question: Can it validate MCP configuration files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Python validator that checks structure, URL schemes, paths, and possible hardcoded secrets.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. The content is educational, but MCP configs can execute commands and use credentials.
